text: The surgeon begins by detaching the omentum from the ileocolic area using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon performs the procedure using a three-port laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon uses a left paraumbilical 10mm trocar for the camera, providing visualization of ICG, along with a right lower quadrant 5mm trocar and a suprapubic 12mm trocar for instrument access.
The surgeon inspects the diseased area and begins to run the small bowel, examining the intestinal loops for any abnormalities.
The surgeon runs the small bowel proximally, revealing disease at the ileocecal valve and an associated fistula. No other evidence of small bowel disease is visible.
The surgeon reveals the fistula in the ileocolic area, which is visually identifiable in the surgical field.

The surgeon encounters a fistula tethered directly to the inferior surface of the ileocecal valve, which was not visible on pre-operative imaging. To improve exposure, lateral immobilization is performed.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization to increase the mobility of the fistulized area, preparing to place a stapler across it. The tissue is lifted higher off the right iliac fossa for better access.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions to bring the fistulized area to a point, preparing to divide the fistula using a stapler.

The surgeon positions an instrument across the targeted area and prepares to staple from the suprapubic port, with the left hand guiding the stapler and the right hand holding a ligature through the created window.
The surgeon positions the stapler underneath the targeted tissue and proceeds to close and fire the device, securing the tissue with staples.

The surgeon continues to separate the innocent bystander loop from the inflammatory phlegmon in the right iliac fossa. The staple line is inspected, showing the bowel loop was stapled across in an anti-mesenteric orientation without impingement or encroachment on the bowel lumen.
The surgeon identifies another loop of bowel stuck inferior to the fistulized loop and begins to expose it, assessing whether it is a second fistula.

The surgeon lifts the distal root of the small bowel mesentery off the retroperitoneum, ensuring cranial mobilization. Dissection occurs in an area away from the ureter, with attempts to visualize its proximity.
The surgeon carefully dissects back and forth from different angles near the right iliac fossa, attempting to approach the area from an inferior angle due to concerns about the ureter's location.
The surgeon approaches the area of adherence in the right iliac fossa from a superior lateral angle, dissecting distally from the right colon to address the stuck tissue.
The surgeon dissects through an unviolated plane, approaching the surgical site from a superior angle, making the dissection process easier.
The surgeon identifies the ureter above the diseased area, using visual cues to locate it without the aid of ICG within the ureters.

The surgeon lifts a separate loop of small bowel off the retroperitoneum, marking an area with a clip for later examination after observing a separation that initially raised concerns about a fistula.

The surgeon attempts to free the valve stuck to the iliac fossa by bluntly dissecting through the chronic phlegmon. A medial approach is chosen to facilitate easier dissection from underneath the affected area.

The surgeon approaches inferior to the ileocolic vessels, entering the scarred retroperitoneal plane. They thin out the pedicle and sweep the duodenum down, creating a window underneath the mesentery beneath the ileocolic vessel before taking the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon dissects and removes the remaining mesentery attached to the colon margin, while preserving the right colic artery in the process.

The surgeon observes adherence from an inferior angle and attempts to elevate the tissue off the retroperitoneum. The dissection continues cautiously due to uncertainty about the ureter's location, but a medial dissection window is found, allowing the surgeon to safely separate the tissue without involving the ureter.

The surgeon creates a window at the planned proximal margin of the thickened small bowel mesentery. The two windows in the mesentery are joined in the thickest area, preparing to use the vessel sealer for further dissection.

The surgeon identifies a window in the mesentery, preparing to take the remaining tissue. The mesentery is carefully managed to prevent delayed or ongoing bleeding, with the vessel sealer effectively ligating the area.

The surgeon prepares for intracorporeal anastomosis by aligning the stapler across the planned colon margin. Indocyanine green (ICG) is administered, and the small bowel is stapled using the suprapubic trocar.
The surgeon places the specimen in the pelvis after considering trocar placement options, including using a suprapubic camera and stapling through the left parumbilical port for optimal angles.
The surgeon lines up the isoparastaltic intracorporeal anastomosis using vascular load staplers to minimize bleeding, aiming to create a small common channel.
The surgeon completes the first inner row of sutures on the common channel using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, sewing from the patient's toes towards the head. The suture line is then invaginated back down.

The surgeon completes the suture line beyond the initial starting point and places a clip for added security. The anastomosis is covered with healthy omentum. The suprapubic port site is upsized to a fan steel incision for specimen extraction.
